content.text: Every significant action an agentic AI system takes — sending an LLM request, invoking a tool, reading or writing to memory, making a planning decision — can cause lasting side effects in the world. Audit logging is the practice of recording those actions as structured, immutable events so that they can be reconstructed, queried, and verified after the fact. Unlike application logs that capture runtime errors and performance metrics, audit logs capture
: who initiated the agent run, what the agent decided to do, what data it accessed, and what the outcomes were.

In traditional software, audit trails focus on human actors — users logging in, editing records, exporting data. In agentic AI, the primary actor is the agent itself. The audit log must capture the AI's reasoning steps alongside its observable side effects because agents are non-deterministic: the same code and the same prompt can produce different sequences of tool calls across runs. Without an audit trail, there is no reliable post-hoc way to answer the question of what the agent did and why.

Audit logging for agents captures events in six categories: LLM requests and responses (including the model, token counts, and stop reason), tool invocations and their results, memory and state modifications, planning and routing decisions, errors and retries, and agent lifecycle events (start, end, pause, handoff). Each event is time-stamped, tagged with a
that groups all events in one agent execution, and attributed to an initiating actor.

Agentic systems are increasingly deployed in contexts where their actions have real-world consequences: they book appointments, send emails, modify code repositories, query sensitive databases, or execute financial transactions. When something goes wrong — or when an auditor or regulator asks what happened — the audit log is the only authoritative record.

is the most pressing driver. Frameworks like HIPAA (healthcare data access), GDPR (personal data processing), SOC 2 (security controls), and PCI-DSS (payment data) mandate complete, tamper-resistant logs of data access and modification. An agent that reads patient records or processes payment details without an audit trail creates legal liability for its operator.
Beyond compliance, audit logs serve three practical purposes. First, they make
possible: when an agent produces an incorrect or harmful output, the audit log lets engineers reconstruct the exact sequence of LLM calls and tool invocations that led there. Second, they enable
: operators can verify that the agent stayed within its intended scope. Third, they provide data for
: analyzing which tool calls succeed or fail and where the agent spends the most tokens.
Every LLM call, tool invocation, and state change is linked by a shared
, making it possible to reconstruct an agent's exact execution path minutes or months after it ran.
Audit logs satisfy the data-access and data-processing logging requirements of HIPAA, GDPR, SOC 2, PCI-DSS, and similar frameworks without requiring architectural changes to the agent itself.

Non-deterministic agent failures are nearly impossible to reproduce. An audit log captures the exact inputs, LLM responses, and tool results at the moment of failure, making root-cause analysis tractable.
Operators can inspect what the agent did, verify it stayed within its sanctioned scope, and intervene or roll back if necessary — foundational to responsible AI deployment.

Streaming audit events to a SIEM enables real-time detection: an agent making an unusually large number of database queries, accessing resources outside its normal scope, or incurring token spend far above baseline.
When multiple agents collaborate, audit logs establish which agent took which action, preventing diffusion of accountability across a shared execution context.

Audit logging adds overhead to every agent operation. Synchronous writes to a slow store can add tens or hundreds of milliseconds to each logged event. The standard mitigation — asynchronous buffered writes — introduces a different risk: a process crash before the buffer is flushed can silently lose audit events. The choice between synchronous durability and asynchronous performance is a genuine trade-off that must be made explicitly based on compliance requirements.

Storage costs grow quickly. A single agentic run making twenty LLM calls, each with multi-kilobyte request and response payloads, produces roughly 100–500 KB of raw log data. At thousands of runs per hour, this accumulates into terabytes per month. Storing full LLM payloads indefinitely is prohibitively expensive; truncating payloads reduces storage costs but limits forensic detail.

Agent inputs and outputs frequently contain personal data. A naive implementation that logs full payloads stores that data in the audit trail, creating a new attack surface. Careful field-level redaction or hashing is required before writing.

Audit log schemas ossify over time. Adding a new field is easy; removing or renaming a field breaks consumers that depend on the old schema. Forward-compatible schema design and versioned schemas are necessary from the start.

A naive append-only table in a relational database degrades for analytical queries beyond a few hundred million rows. Column-oriented stores (ClickHouse, Redshift) or partitioned S3-backed query engines (Athena, BigQuery) are necessary for production-scale analytics.
Incomplete coverage is worse than partial coverage.

An audit log that captures tool calls but omits LLM requests gives a false sense of accountability. All significant agent actions must be captured, or none should be presented as a compliance-grade audit trail.

that intercepts agent actions before and after execution. This is typically implemented as a middleware wrapper, a decorator applied to the agent's tool executor, or a callback hook registered with the agent framework. Regardless of the implementation style, the layer performs four operations on every event:
the raw event with contextual metadata: a globally unique
shared across the run, the current timestamp in ISO 8601 format, the agent version, and the actor's identity. Second, it
fields known to contain PII. Third, it
the enriched event to JSON. Fourth, it
the event for asynchronous delivery to the log store, returning control to the agent immediately.
A well-designed audit event schema includes:
(UUID v4 shared by all events in one run),
(tool name, DB table, or API endpoint),
The audit capture layer should be positioned as close to the agent's action execution as possible — not at the HTTP transport layer where context is already lost. Wrapping the agent's
method directly is the correct level of abstraction.

attrs.alt: Audit logging architecture: Agent Runtime emits events to the Audit Capture Layer which enriches, redacts, and serializes them, then enqueues to an Async Log Buffer that fans out to a Structured Log Store and a SIEM/Alert Platform, both queryable by operators.

content.text: produces four categories of auditable action: LLM request/response pairs, tool calls and their results, memory and state operations, and planning decisions. Each carries implicit context — actor, trace ID, timestamp — that the instrumentation layer uses to build structured events.

(amber) is a thin middleware or decorator that intercepts every agent action, enriches it with event metadata, redacts PII, serializes to JSON, and asynchronously enqueues the event. This layer should add less than one millisecond of overhead per operation.
(green) decouples write latency from agent execution — an in-memory asyncio queue, a Kafka topic, or AWS Kinesis stream. It fans out to two downstream consumers: the
for real-time alerting, both queryable by operators and compliance officers.

Audit logging is not optional in most production agentic deployments. Use it whenever: the agent has access to production data or can modify external systems; the agent acts on behalf of users in a regulated industry; the agent can take irreversible actions (send emails, delete files, process payments); a compliance framework requires data-access logging; or the agent operates in a multi-tenant environment where tenant isolation must be verifiable after the fact.
The question is not whether to audit log but
to capture. Most production systems use a tiered approach: full payloads for high-risk action types (database writes, external API calls), and metadata-only for routine read operations.
tool calls that modify external state, LLM requests that access personal data, agent lifecycle events, and authorization decisions.
LLM inputs and outputs that may contain user-supplied content; store token counts and truncated previews rather than full payloads.
high-frequency read operations (memory lookups, vector similarity searches) where full payloads would create storage problems without proportional compliance benefit.
internal computational steps with no external side effects, pure in-process transformations, and test/development environments.
Define an event schema with all mandatory fields as a Python dataclass or Pydantic model so it is type-checked and enforceable in code.
Identify every instrumentation point in the agent's execution path: the LLM call site, each tool executor, each memory read/write, and the agent start/end boundaries.
Implement the audit capture layer as a context manager or decorator wrapping each instrumentation point. Use Python's
through async call stacks without explicit parameter threading.
Implement PII redaction before serialization: maintain a set of field names that must be hashed (
) and apply redaction recursively to nested dicts before the event is serialized.
Create an async writer backed by an
with a background drain task. Set a maximum queue size with a documented backpressure policy (block or drop-and-alert).
Set up the log store: PostgreSQL with BRIN index on
for low-to-medium volume; ClickHouse or partitioned Parquet on S3 for analytics at scale.
Add a retention policy: define hot store duration, cold archival period, and deletion date; implement it as a database lifecycle rule.
Integrate real-time alerting: forward high-severity event types to a SIEM or PagerDuty, batch routine events for bulk delivery.

# Minimal audit logger for an agentic AI system
# pip install anthropic pydantic

import uuid
import hashlib
import asyncio
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from contextvars import ContextVar
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict, field
from typing import Any, Optional
import json

# --- Trace context propagation ---
_trace_id: ContextVar[str] = ContextVar("trace_id", default="")
_actor: ContextVar[str] = ContextVar("actor", default="unknown")


def new_trace(actor: str) -> str:
    """Call at the start of each agent run to set the trace context."""
    trace = str(uuid.uuid4())
    _trace_id.set(trace)
    _actor.set(actor)
    return trace


@dataclass
class AuditEvent:
    event_type: str          # llm_request, tool_call, agent_start, error, ...
    resource: str            # model name, tool name, or DB table
    outcome: str             # success, error, timeout
    payload: dict            # redacted inputs and outputs
    event_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    trace_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: _trace_id.get())
    actor: str = field(default_factory=lambda: _actor.get())
    timestamp: str = field(
        default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    )
    latency_ms: Optional[float] = None
    metadata: dict = field(default_factory=dict)


# --- PII redaction ---
PII_FIELDS = {"email", "user_name", "ssn", "phone", "dob", "ip_address"}


def redact(obj: Any) -> Any:
    """Recursively hash PII fields; leave other values unchanged."""
    if isinstance(obj, dict):
        return {
            k: (
                "sha256:" + hashlib.sha256(str(v).encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
                if k in PII_FIELDS and v is not None
                else redact(v)
            )
            for k, v in obj.items()
        }
    if isinstance(obj, list):
        return [redact(item) for item in obj]
    return obj


class AuditLogger:
    def __init__(self):
        self._queue: asyncio.Queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=10_000)

    async def start(self):
        asyncio.create_task(self._writer_loop())

    async def _writer_loop(self):
        while True:
            event: AuditEvent = await self._queue.get()
            # Production: write to ClickHouse / PostgreSQL / Kafka
            print(json.dumps(asdict(event), indent=2))
            self._queue.task_done()

    async def emit(self, event: AuditEvent):
        event.payload = redact(event.payload)
        try:
            self._queue.put_nowait(event)
        except asyncio.QueueFull:
            print(json.dumps({"event_type": "log_overflow", "dropped": event.event_id}))

    async def flush(self):
        await self._queue.join()


async def main():
    logger = AuditLogger()
    await logger.start()

    trace = new_trace(actor="user:alice")

    import time
    start = time.monotonic()

    await logger.emit(AuditEvent(
        event_type="tool_call",
        resource="customer_lookup",
        outcome="success",
        payload={
            # PII fields are automatically hashed by redact()
            "query": {"email": "alice@example.com", "account_id": "acct-123"},
            "result": {"record_count": 1, "status": "active"},
        },
        latency_ms=round((time.monotonic() - start) * 1000, 2),
    ))

    await logger.flush()


asyncio.run(main())
Middleware Wrapper for the Tool Executor

# Audit middleware: wraps an agent's tool executor transparently.
# All tool calls emit structured audit events without modifying tool code.

import asyncio, time, uuid, json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from contextvars import ContextVar
from typing import Any, Callable, Optional

_trace_id: ContextVar[str] = ContextVar("trace_id", default="no-trace")
_actor: ContextVar[str] = ContextVar("actor", default="system")


@dataclass
class AuditEvent:
    event_type: str
    resource: str
    outcome: str
    payload: dict
    event_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    trace_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: _trace_id.get())
    actor: str = field(default_factory=lambda: _actor.get())
    timestamp: str = field(
        default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    )
    latency_ms: float = 0.0


class AuditedToolExecutor:
    """Wraps tool callables; emits a structured audit event on every call."""

    def __init__(self, tools: dict[str, Callable], emit_fn: Callable,
                 max_payload_bytes: int = 4096):
        self._tools = tools
        self._emit = emit_fn
        self._max_bytes = max_payload_bytes

    def _truncate(self, obj: Any) -> Any:
        s = json.dumps(obj, default=str)
        if len(s.encode()) <= self._max_bytes:
            return obj
        return {"_truncated": True, "_preview": s[:256] + "..."}

    async def call(self, tool_name: str, tool_input: dict) -> Any:
        if tool_name not in self._tools:
            raise ValueError(f"Unknown tool: {tool_name!r}")

        t0 = time.monotonic()
        outcome, result, error_msg = "success", None, None

        try:
            fn = self._tools[tool_name]
            result = (
                await fn(**tool_input)
                if asyncio.iscoroutinefunction(fn)
                else fn(**tool_input)
            )
        except Exception as exc:
            outcome = "error"
            error_msg = f"{type(exc).__name__}: {exc}"
            raise
        finally:
            latency = round((time.monotonic() - t0) * 1000, 2)
            payload = {"input": self._truncate(tool_input)}
            if result is not None:
                payload["output"] = self._truncate(result)
            if error_msg:
                payload["error"] = error_msg

            await self._emit(AuditEvent(
                event_type="tool_call",
                resource=tool_name,
                outcome=outcome,
                payload=payload,
                latency_ms=latency,
            ))

        return result


async def demo():
    events: list[dict] = []

    async def collect(event: AuditEvent):
        events.append(asdict(event))

    async def web_search(query: str) -> list[str]:
        return [f"Result for: {query}"]

    def get_weather(city: str) -> dict:
        return {"city": city, "temp_c": 18, "condition": "cloudy"}

    _trace_id.set(str(uuid.uuid4()))
    _actor.set("user:bob")

    executor = AuditedToolExecutor(
        tools={"web_search": web_search, "get_weather": get_weather},
        emit_fn=collect,
    )

    await executor.call("web_search", {"query": "Python 3.13 release date"})
    await executor.call("get_weather", {"city": "London"})

    for e in events:
        print(json.dumps(e, indent=2))


asyncio.run(demo())

Consider a healthcare agent deployed at a clinic. The agent accepts natural-language queries from physicians — "summarize all lab results for patient 47382 in the past 90 days" — queries the electronic health record (EHR) system, synthesizes a structured summary using an LLM, and returns it to the physician's dashboard. Under HIPAA, every access to patient records must be logged with the identity of the accessor, the records accessed, and the timestamp.

The compliance requirement translates into an audit log specification: every EHR query event must record the patient ID (hashed — the log store is not a medical system and should not store raw patient identifiers), the physician ID, the number of records returned, and the agent version. The LLM summary content is not stored because it may contain PHI; instead, a SHA-256 hash of the summary is stored alongside its token count, which is sufficient to prove a summary was generated and detect tampering.

# Healthcare agent with HIPAA-compliant audit logging
import asyncio, hashlib, uuid, time, json
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from contextvars import ContextVar

_trace_id: ContextVar[str] = ContextVar("trace_id", default="")
_actor: ContextVar[str] = ContextVar("actor", default="unknown")


@dataclass
class AuditEvent:
    event_type: str
    resource: str
    outcome: str
    payload: dict
    event_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    trace_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: _trace_id.get())
    actor: str = field(default_factory=lambda: _actor.get())
    timestamp: str = field(
        default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    )
    latency_ms: float = 0.0
    metadata: dict = field(default_factory=dict)


def sha256_prefix(value: str, length: int = 16) -> str:
    return "sha256:" + hashlib.sha256(value.encode()).hexdigest()[:length]


class HIPAAAuditLogger:
    """Append-only logger enforcing HIPAA field requirements."""

    def __init__(self, store_fn=None):
        self._store = store_fn or (lambda e: print(json.dumps(e, indent=2)))
        self._queue: asyncio.Queue = asyncio.Queue()

    async def start(self):
        asyncio.create_task(self._drain())

    async def _drain(self):
        while True:
            event = await self._queue.get()
            await self._store(asdict(event))
            self._queue.task_done()

    async def log_ehr_access(self, patient_id: str, physician_id: str,
                             record_count: int, latency_ms: float):
        await self._queue.put(AuditEvent(
            event_type="ehr_read",
            resource="ehr_system",
            outcome="success",
            latency_ms=latency_ms,
            payload={
                # Hash patient ID — HIPAA prohibits storing PHI in log
                # systems that lack full PHI security controls.
                "patient_id_hash": sha256_prefix(patient_id),
                "physician_id": physician_id,
                "record_count": record_count,
            },
            metadata={"compliance": "HIPAA"},
        ))

    async def log_llm_summary(self, summary: str, token_count: int,
                              model: str, latency_ms: float):
        await self._queue.put(AuditEvent(
            event_type="llm_response",
            resource=model,
            outcome="success",
            latency_ms=latency_ms,
            payload={
                # Store hash, not content — summary may contain PHI
                "summary_hash": sha256_prefix(summary),
                "summary_tokens": token_count,
            },
            metadata={"compliance": "HIPAA"},
        ))


async def run_healthcare_agent(patient_id: str, physician_id: str) -> str:
    _trace_id.set(str(uuid.uuid4()))
    _actor.set(physician_id)

    logger = HIPAAAuditLogger()
    await logger.start()

    t0 = time.monotonic()
    records = [{"date": "2025-06-01", "type": "CBC", "result": "normal"}] * 5
    await logger.log_ehr_access(
        patient_id=patient_id,
        physician_id=physician_id,
        record_count=len(records),
        latency_ms=round((time.monotonic() - t0) * 1000, 2),
    )

    t1 = time.monotonic()
    summary = "Five lab results retrieved. All CBC values within normal range."
    await logger.log_llm_summary(
        summary=summary,
        token_count=42,
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        latency_ms=round((time.monotonic() - t1) * 1000, 2),
    )

    await asyncio.sleep(0.05)
    return summary


asyncio.run(run_healthcare_agent("patient-47382", "physician-dr-chen"))

writes serialized events to a ClickHouse table partitioned by month. Events older than 90 days move to S3-backed cold storage via a lifecycle rule; events older than six years (the HIPAA retention minimum) are deleted. The compliance officer queries ClickHouse by
for monthly access reports, and by
to identify all access events for a specific patient when responding to a right-of-access request.
The most common gap is logging only failures. A system that records

for failed tool calls but nothing for successful ones looks clean even when the agent accessed thousands of sensitive records without incident. Regulators and security auditors require a complete record of all access events. Every significant action must be logged regardless of outcome.
An audit log without a shared
across all events in one agent run is nearly useless for forensic investigation. Without a grouping key, there is no way to reconstruct which tool call preceded which LLM call. Use Python's
to propagate the trace ID through the entire async call stack without explicit threading through every function parameter.

Storing unredacted user messages, email addresses, patient identifiers, or authentication tokens in the audit log creates a second attack surface with higher-severity data breach consequences. Audit log databases are often given broader access than application databases because compliance teams query them — making a breach of the audit log particularly damaging. Apply field-level redaction or hashing
the event leaves the capture layer.
Synchronous Writes on the Critical Path

Writing audit events synchronously inside the same transaction as the agent action couples log write latency to agent latency. If the log database is slow or unavailable, the agent blocks or fails. Asynchronous writes with a local buffer are the correct pattern: the agent enqueues the event and continues immediately; a background task drains the queue to the durable store.

Audit logs that grow without bound eventually exceed storage budgets and query performance thresholds. Define the retention period at deployment time, implement archival to cold storage after the hot retention window, and document the deletion schedule in the data-processing agreement.
Log at the agent action level, not the HTTP level.

HTTP-level request logging captures network events but loses agent context: the trace ID, the tool name, the actor identity. Instrument the agent code directly so every log event carries full semantic context.
once at agent run start and it is automatically available in every coroutine in that run, including those spawned with

Use a Pydantic model or dataclass for audit events. Reject events that do not conform rather than silently dropping fields. A schema enforcement error is far easier to debug than discovering months later that half of events are missing the

Truncate LLM inputs and outputs to a configurable maximum (2–8 KB) and hash or omit all PII fields. Store full payloads only when forensic depth is genuinely required, in a separately access-controlled store.
Separate audit log storage from application storage.
The audit log should be in a different database instance. This prevents an application-level breach from silently corrupting the audit trail.
Grant the agent's service account INSERT permission only — no UPDATE or DELETE. Use immutable S3 object locks or ClickHouse merge-tree append semantics to prevent after-the-fact modification.
Instrument the background log writer to emit a
metric to monitoring when delivery fails. A gap in the audit trail is a security incident in itself.

A correctly implemented audit logger adds negligible overhead to the agent's critical path. The hot path — construct the event dataclass, apply redaction, serialize to JSON, enqueue to the asyncio queue — consistently completes in under 200 microseconds per event. This is imperceptible compared to the hundreds of milliseconds consumed by LLM API calls and external tool invocations.
The background writer introduces the main operational concern:

. If the log store becomes slow or unavailable, events accumulate in the in-memory queue. Implement a maximum queue size with a backpressure policy: either block the agent (for regulated deployments where losing audit events is worse than adding latency) or drop the oldest events and emit a

Send events in batches of 50–500 rather than one at a time. ClickHouse and Kafka both perform significantly better with batch inserts. A 1–5 second batch interval is typically sufficient for compliance latency requirements.

PostgreSQL handles point-in-time queries well but degrades for analytical aggregates beyond a few hundred million rows. ClickHouse or Parquet on S3 queried via Athena are 10–100x faster for aggregate queries over billions of events.
Partition the audit log by time (monthly or weekly) and by
. Dropping the oldest partition for retention policy enforcement is a metadata operation, not a row-by-row delete.

In very high-throughput pipelines, always log all events of high-risk types (writes, deletions, auth events) and sample routine read events at a configurable rate (1–10%). Document the sampling rate explicitly so auditors understand the coverage.
Vendor-neutral, standard context propagation, trace + log correlation
Unifying audit logging with distributed tracing
Extremely fast aggregations, native JSON support, append-only merge trees
High-volume log analytics and compliance dashboards
SQL familiarity, ACID guarantees, time-series hypertables
Low-to-medium volume, teams already running PostgreSQL
Cheapest storage, Parquet compression, serverless SQL
Long-term archival and infrequent analytical queries
Durable ordered log, fan-out to multiple consumers, replay
High-throughput fan-out to store and SIEM simultaneously
Integrated tracing + logs, ML anomaly detection, dashboards
Teams already on Datadog wanting minimal infrastructure
Powerful SPL search language, compliance report templates
Full-text search across payload content, Kibana dashboards
Teams needing full-text search over log payloads

Distributed tracing and audit logging are complementary. Tracing captures performance and causal structure — what called what and how long it took. Audit logging captures the compliance and accountability record — who did what to which resource with what result. Production agentic systems benefit from both.

Event sourcing is philosophically similar — it also builds an immutable log of events — but its primary purpose is state reconstruction rather than accountability. Event-sourced systems often serve double duty as audit logs, but they incur higher write amplification and schema complexity than a purpose-built audit log.
The audit log is only as trustworthy as its protection from tampering. An attacker who can modify or delete audit events can erase evidence of their own actions. Four controls are necessary:

means the agent's service account has INSERT permission on the audit log table but no UPDATE or DELETE — enforced at the database level, not in application code. On S3, use Object Lock in COMPLIANCE mode with a retention period matching the legal requirement.

means the audit log receives events through a dedicated write path distinct from the application's data path: separate database credentials, a separate Kafka topic with its own IAM policy, or a dedicated S3 bucket. An attacker who compromises the application database cannot reach the audit log through the same credentials.
Each audit event can include a

field containing the SHA-256 hash of the previous event's serialized content. Deletion of a middle event breaks the chain and makes the deletion detectable. For regulated environments, prefer a purpose-built tamper-evident log service (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log) over hand-rolled hash chaining.
Encrypt at rest and in transit.

Enforce AES-256 at rest (enabled by default on most managed databases and S3) and TLS 1.3 between the agent and the log buffer and between the buffer and the store. Use separate KMS keys for the audit log so key compromise is scoped independently.

A log store unavailable during an incident creates a gap in the audit trail precisely when it is most needed. Use synchronous replication to at least two availability zones. If the store is unavailable, queue events in durable local storage (SQLite or a local Kafka partition) rather than dropping them.
Access control to the log store.

Restrict read access to compliance officers, security engineers, and automated alerting systems. Do not grant developers broad read access — create curated queries and views returning only the fields needed for specific debugging tasks.
metric to a separate monitoring channel when delivery fails. A gap in the audit trail is a security incident in itself and must be detected automatically.
Audit logging for agentic AI records every significant agent action — LLM calls, tool invocations, memory operations, planning decisions — as structured, immutable events linked by a shared
, enabling after-the-fact reconstruction of exactly what the agent did and why.
through the entire async call stack without changing every function signature; set it once at agent run start and it is automatically available in every coroutine in that run.
Always write audit events asynchronously through a buffer to keep log write latency off the agent's critical path; the total async overhead per event is consistently under 200 microseconds.

Apply PII redaction — SHA-256 hashing or sentinel replacement — before the event leaves the capture layer; storing raw personal data in the audit log creates a second compliance liability and a higher-value breach target.

For log storage, choose based on query pattern: PostgreSQL for low-to-medium volume, ClickHouse or partitioned Parquet on S3 for analytics over billions of events; always enforce append-only access at the database permission level.

Protect the log store through write-path separation with distinct credentials, append-only database permissions, and out-of-band backup; an attacker who can modify audit events can erase evidence of their own actions.

Audit logging and distributed tracing (OTEL) are complementary: tracing provides performance and causal structure; audit logging provides compliance and accountability; production agentic deployments benefit from both.

Define a retention policy at deployment time — hot store duration, cold archival period, and deletion date — and implement it as a lifecycle rule; undocumented audit data accumulates indefinitely and creates both storage costs and legal uncertainty.
